title: Fall of the Assad regime in Syria
summary: In the early hours of 8 December 2024, less than twelve days after the beginning of an unexpected rebel offensive out of Idlib, fighters of Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham (HTS) and allied factions entered Damascus. President Bashar al-Assad, whose family had ruled Syria for fifty-four years and who had survived thirteen years of civil war with the massive intervention of Russia, Iran and Hezbollah, boarded a Russian aircraft that took him to Moscow, where he was granted asylum. Crowds tore down statues of his father Hafez, freed prisoners from the Saydnaya military prison, and gathered in the Umayyad Mosque courtyard. In under two weeks the rebel column had taken Aleppo (30 November), Hama (5 December), Homs (7 December) and the capital itself. Ahmed al-Sharaa, better known to the world by his wartime nom de guerre Abu Mohammad al-Jolani, emerged from Idlib to lead a transitional Syrian government. The Islamic Republic's 'axis of resistance' lost its most important Arab state ally; Russia's naval and air bases at Tartus and Hmeimim were suddenly precarious; Turkey found its long-standing bet on the Syrian opposition paying off. Israel used the vacuum to move troops into a buffer zone in Syrian Quneitra. The Syrian civil war, which by 2024 had killed at least 600,000 people and displaced more than half the population, appeared - for the moment - to be over.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Bashar al-Assad in May 2024, seven months before his flight from Damascus.

sections.body:
The Ba'athist state that fell on 8 December 2024 had been built by Hafez al-Assad, an Alawite air-force officer who seized power in a coup on 13 November 1970 and ruled Syria until his death in June 2000. His younger son Bashar, a London-trained ophthalmologist who had never been intended for high office until the death of his elder brother Basil in a 1994 car crash, inherited the presidency at the age of thirty-four. The initial 'Damascus Spring' of political openness was brief. Fifteen years of increasingly repressive rule culminated, in March 2011, in the arrest and torture of a group of schoolchildren in the southern town of Daraa for scrawling anti-regime graffiti. The nationwide protests that followed, met with live ammunition, escalated into an armed uprising and then into a full-scale civil war.

At its lowest point in the summer of 2015 the Assad government controlled only the Damascus-Homs-Latakia corridor. The intervention of Russian air power from 30 September 2015, and of Iranian Revolutionary Guards Corps advisers and Hezbollah ground troops from earlier, together with regime scorched-earth tactics, systematic sieges and, on multiple occasions documented by the OPCW, the use of chemical weapons, gradually reversed the tide. By 2020 Damascus had recovered most of the country except for Kurdish-controlled north-east Syria, a Turkish-controlled belt across the north, and Idlib province in the north-west, where a rebel enclave under the dominant local faction Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham survived under Turkish protection and Russian-brokered ceasefire.

By 2024 the Assad regime was widely regarded as having 'won' the war and had been readmitted to the Arab League. But it had won a hollow victory. The Syrian pound had lost more than 99 percent of its 2011 value. State salaries had been reduced to the equivalent of a few dollars a month. Electricity supply in Damascus was down to two or three hours a day. The country's youth had emigrated in the millions. The captagon methamphetamine trade, run out of ports and airfields associated with regime figures, had become one of the state's most valuable exports. Above all, Assad's foreign patrons were distracted. Russia was consumed by its war in Ukraine and had drawn down its Syrian forces. Iran and Hezbollah were reeling from Israel's Lebanese campaign of September-October 2024, in which Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah and much of the Iranian-backed axis's senior military leadership had been killed.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: HTS and allied fighters with their pickup trucks in Aleppo, December 2024, during the offensive against Assad's forces.

sections.body:
The offensive began at dawn on 27 November 2024. A coalition led by Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham, the successor organisation to the al-Qaeda-affiliated Jabhat al-Nusra that had rebranded itself, expelled its most doctrinaire foreign fighters, and reorganised as a would-be governing body in Idlib, moved out of its enclave with the Turkish-backed Syrian National Army in support. The rebels advanced on Aleppo along the M5 highway from the west. Syrian government defensive lines, thinned by years of desertion and by the withdrawal of Iranian and Hezbollah troops, collapsed almost immediately. Aleppo, Syria's second city and the object of some of the most brutal fighting of the earlier war, fell in three days; HTS entered the city on 29-30 November, taking the citadel and the airport with minimal resistance. Russian aircraft launched airstrikes on the advancing column but did not slow it.

Hama, the next major city south along the M5, fell on 5 December, with the local Syrian Arab Army garrison surrendering after brief clashes. Homs, the strategic crossroads controlling access to Damascus and to the Alawite coastal heartland, fell on 7 December after Assad's forces largely abandoned their positions overnight. In the south, meanwhile, an independent 'Southern Operations Room' of local Daraa and Suwayda-based factions moved north and took Daraa on 6 December. By the night of 7-8 December two rebel columns were converging on the capital from north and south, while, in the countryside around Damascus, other factions had already emerged from years of low-level dormancy to take positions around the city.

In the early hours of 8 December Bashar al-Assad boarded a Russian aircraft at Damascus airport bound for Moscow. Prime Minister Mohammad Ghazi al-Jalali released a video from his office pledging to co-operate with 'the leadership chosen by the people'. Fighters entered the presidential palace, filmed themselves in Assad's private rooms, and made off with symbolic loot. At Saydnaya prison, north of Damascus, the notorious 'human slaughterhouse' where Amnesty International estimated tens of thousands had been executed under Assad, fighters and civilian volunteers freed thousands of prisoners; images of gaunt men emerging into daylight for the first time in years dominated global coverage. By late morning HTS commander Ahmed al-Sharaa entered the Umayyad Mosque, one of Islam's oldest and most sacred sites, and addressed the crowds. Fifty-four years of Assad-family rule had ended in a weekend. The Kremlin confirmed later on 8 December that Assad had 'left the presidency' and been granted asylum in Russia.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Syrians in central Damascus celebrating the fall of the Assad regime, December 2024.

sections.body:
Ahmed al-Sharaa moved rapidly to consolidate authority. Within days of the takeover he had appointed Mohammed al-Bashir, previously prime minister of HTS's Idlib-based Syrian Salvation Government, as caretaker prime minister of Syria. He established transitional bodies for constitutional reform and for the security services, and moved to disband the old army and intelligence agencies while attempting to absorb their manpower. In January 2025 he was named president of Syria for a transitional period. He replaced HTS's distinctive black-and-white flag with the three-starred green-white-black tricolour of the Syrian opposition, which was raised over official buildings, and began appearing in tailored jackets rather than in fatigues. Foreign delegations began arriving in Damascus almost immediately - from Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, the European Union, the United States - and moved, over the first half of 2025, to lift sanctions and to reopen embassies.

On Syria's borders, the fall of the regime prompted immediate reactions. Israel, arguing that the Syrian army was no longer capable of enforcing the 1974 disengagement agreement, moved troops into and beyond the demilitarised buffer zone on the Golan Heights and destroyed the bulk of the abandoned Syrian air force, navy and heavy weapons in a wave of airstrikes over 8-10 December. Turkey moved its Syrian National Army proxies against Kurdish-held areas in the north-east, in a campaign that continued through the early months of 2025. Russian forces evacuated much of their equipment from Hmeimim air base and Tartus naval base and negotiated the terms of a reduced presence with the new authorities. Iran and Hezbollah, having lost Syria as a land bridge, ceased their overland arms flows to Lebanon.

Inside Syria the picture was mixed. Reprisals against former regime figures were reported in the Alawite coastal region, particularly in March 2025, when hundreds of Alawite civilians were killed in an outbreak of sectarian violence around Latakia and Baniyas. Investigations of the Assad-era archives, some of them released to journalists, prosecutors and to families of the disappeared, promised to shed light on decades of enforced disappearance; the Syrian Network for Human Rights reported that more than 130,000 Syrians had been detained or disappeared under Assad. Economic recovery was uneven and slow; several million Syrian refugees, particularly from Turkey and Lebanon, began returning.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The fall of the Assad regime marked the end of the last surviving Ba'athist state in the Arab world - Saddam Hussein's Iraqi Ba'ath had been destroyed by the American invasion of 2003 - and one of the most durable authoritarian dynasties of the modern Middle East. It also completed a wider unravelling of what Iran had for two decades called its 'Axis of Resistance'. In the space of fourteen months from October 2023, Hamas's political and military leadership in Gaza had been decimated, Hezbollah's leadership in Lebanon had been eliminated by Israeli strikes, and now the Syrian regime that had linked Tehran to the eastern Mediterranean was gone. Iran's regional deterrence, which had once seemed formidable, looked, in early 2025, in tatters.

For Russia the loss of Syria was among the most significant strategic setbacks of the Putin era. The Hmeimim air base and the Tartus naval facility had, since the 2015 intervention, been Russia's only Mediterranean footholds and its principal logistical hubs for the projection of power into Africa. Their future was, as of mid-2025, still being negotiated with the al-Sharaa government. The failure of Russia's Syria bet also raised doubts, in capitals from Havana to Yerevan, about the value of Russian security guarantees.

For Syrians themselves the moment held both immense promise and immense risk. The country the rebels inherited was in ruin: cities destroyed, half the population displaced, economy shattered, sectarian and ethnic wounds unhealed. HTS, though it had spent years positioning itself as a Syrian nationalist governing force rather than a jihadist militia, retained a designated terrorist organisation status in Washington, London and Brussels well into 2025 before delistings began. Al-Sharaa's own past - a former al-Qaeda commander who had spent years in an American prison in Iraq - remained the object of intense international scrutiny. Whether the transitional government could hold together a diverse Syrian polity - Sunni Arabs, Alawites, Christians, Druze, Kurds - and rebuild functional state institutions was the open question of the country's post-Assad decade.

figures.name: Bashar al-Assad
figures.role: President of Syria from 17 July 2000 until his flight to Moscow on 8 December 2024; granted asylum in Russia

figures.name: Ahmed al-Sharaa
figures.role: Formerly known as Abu Mohammad al-Jolani; leader of Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham, commander of the November-December 2024 offensive, and Syria's transitional president from January 2025

figures.name: Mohammed al-Bashir
figures.role: Former prime minister of the HTS-run Syrian Salvation Government in Idlib; appointed caretaker prime minister of Syria on 9 December 2024

figures.name: Hafez al-Assad
figures.role: Founder of the Alawite Assad dynasty; ruled Syria from November 1970 until his death in June 2000, when he was succeeded by his son Bashar

figures.name: Hassan Nasrallah
figures.role: Long-time secretary-general of Hezbollah, whose killing by an Israeli airstrike in Beirut on 27 September 2024 removed one of Assad's most important remaining allies weeks before the offensive

images.caption: A defaced poster of Bashar al-Assad in Aleppo, days after the city's capture by HTS-led forces in early December 2024.

sources.title: Syria: UN calls for orderly transition after fall of Assad regime
